    Boeing 737

    Boeing · Narrowbody · In production (MAX series)

    The best-selling airliner in history.

    Length39.52 m
    Wingspan35.92 m
    Range6,480 km
    MTOW82 t
    Typical seats178
    Built11,700+
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Seats per metre#16 of 30
    4.50 seats/m typical seats ÷ fuselage length
    Range per tonne#2 of 40
    78.8 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#18 of 43
    0.91 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Boeing 737's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Boeing 73735.92 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #39 of 43 by length → #38 by weight → #29 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length (MAX 8)39.52 m
    Wingspan35.92 m
    Height12.30 m
    MTOW82,191 kg
    Range6,480 km
    Typical seats178 (2-class)
    Maximum seats210
    Engines2 × CFM LEAP-1B
    First flight (MAX)29 January 2016
    Family first flight9 April 1967

    Compare it

    Put the Boeing 737 next to something

    vs COMAC C919 →vs Airbus A220 →
    vs Airbus A380 vs Boeing 777 Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousBoeing 727 Next →Boeing 747

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    No airliner has been built in greater numbers than the 737 — roughly 11,700 across four generations since 1967, with thousands more on order. If you have flown, you have almost certainly flown on one. It is short, low to the ground (a 1960s decision to suit airports with no jet bridges), and unmistakable in profile: the stubby dropped nose and the dorsal fin fillet along the spine.

    That low stance became the family's defining engineering constraint. Every new generation of larger engines had to be squeezed under the same short landing gear — which is why 737 nacelles are famously flattened at the bottom, and why the MAX's bigger LEAP engines had to be mounted further forward and higher.

    The numbers here are for the MAX 8, the current generation's core variant. The family spans everything from the 85-seat -500 to the 230-seat MAX 10.

    Variants

    The Boeing 737 line

    737-100 / -200 (Original)
    Where it all started, 1967.
    737 Classic (-300/-400/-500)
    CFM56 power, 1980s.
    737 NG (-600/-700/-800/-900)
    The definitive generation — the -800 is the Scale Engine reference aircraft.
    737 MAX (7/8/9/10)
    Current generation. MAX 8 spec shown above.
    Operators

    Who flies the Boeing 737

    The top 25 operators by fleet size, of 500+ worldwide — the rest are aggregated below. Counts marked ≈ are approximate, verified 2026-07-14.

    USSouthwest AirlinesThe largest 737 fleet in the world and an all-737 airline: 305 × 737-700, 198 × 737-800 and 300 × 737 MAX 8.803✓
    FRRyanairEurope's biggest airline runs an all-737 fleet.≈600→ UAUnited Airlines≈390→ AAAmerican Airlines≈345→ DLDelta Air Lines≈270→
    USAlaska AirlinesAll-Boeing mainline fleet.≈230
    CZChina Southern Airlines≈190→
    CNShenzhen AirlinesEffectively an all-737 operation.≈170
    CNXiamen AirlinesHistorically an all-Boeing airline.≈160
    CAAir China≈150→
    BRGol Linhas AéreasBrazil's all-737 carrier.≈140
    MUChina Eastern Airlines≈130→
    CNShandong Airlines≈130
    CAWestJet≈110
    PACopa AirlinesThe all-737 hub carrier of the Americas.≈110
    MXAeromexico≈100
    IDLion Air≈100
    AEflydubaiAn all-737 airline built around the MAX.≈90
    GBJet2.com≈90
    NONorwegian≈85
    TKTurkish Airlines≈80→ QFQantasThe 737-800 is the backbone of the domestic network.≈75→
    TRSunExpress≈70
    GBTUI Airways≈60
    NHAll Nippon Airways737-800s on Japanese domestic and regional routes.≈39→
    Other operatorsMore than 475 further airlines worldwide fly the 737 — no airliner has a broader operator base. Counts on this page are fleet-report approximations pending the full verification pass the A380 and A350 lists received.≈2,500+

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
